name: Jotunheim
shortDescription: Jotunheim is the home of the giants (Jötnar) in Norse mythology, a wild and untamed land often associated with chaos and the forces of nature. It is a realm of mountains, forests, and icy wastelands, standing in stark contrast to the more ordered realms of Asgard and Midgard.
description:
Jotunheim, also known as Jötunheimr, is one of the Nine Worlds in Norse cosmology and the primary home of the Jötnar, often translated as giants, though 'troll' or 'monstrous being' might be more accurate depending on the context. It is geographically separated from Asgard, the home of the Aesir gods, and Midgard, the realm of humans. Jotunheim is typically depicted as a harsh and unforgiving land, characterized by rugged mountains, dense, dark forests, icy rivers, and vast, frozen wastelands. It represents the untamed forces of nature, chaos, and the primal state of existence before order was imposed by the gods. 

Several specific locations exist within Jotunheim, including Utgard, a fortress ruled by Utgarda-Loki, known for its illusions and trickery. Other notable locations include Gastropnir, the home of the giantess Gerd, and Thrymheim, the hall of the giant Thrym. Interactions between the gods and the giants in Jotunheim are frequent and often involve conflict, trickery, or the acquisition of valuable items or knowledge. Thor, the god of thunder, is a frequent visitor to Jotunheim, often engaging in battles with the giants to protect Asgard and Midgard. Loki, despite being associated with the Aesir, is of Jotun descent and frequently travels to Jotunheim, often acting as a bridge between the worlds. The creation myth also links Jotunheim to the primordial giant Ymir, from whose body the world was formed, further emphasizing the connection between the realm and primal chaos. The realm serves as a constant reminder of the ever-present threat to the order established by the gods.

location.cosmological: Located to the east and north of Midgard, separated by rivers and dangerous terrain. It represents the wild, untamed areas beyond the ordered realms.

sources.title: The Lost Gods of Northern Europe
sources.author: H.R. Ellis Davidson
